IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This work tackles an important practical problem in multi-task NLP—separating shared vs task-specific features—and proposes a concrete adversarial shared-private framework that extends adversarial training from binary to multi-class task discrimination. Empirical gains across 16 text-classification tasks (14 Amazon domains, IMDB, MR) and the claim that learned shared features transfer to new tasks indicate the approach could change how researchers design MTL systems. The extension to multi-class adversarial discrimination and demonstrated cross-domain improvements are likely to be adopted and extended by others, though the approach largely combines existing ideas rather than introducing a wholly new paradigm. | B(4): The paper tackles the important problem of separating shared vs. task-specific features in multi-task NLP and mitigating negative transfer by proposing an adversarial shared-private MTL framework. Extending adversarial training from binary to multi-class task discrimination is a technically useful advance that enables joint training over many tasks. Empirical evidence of lower error rates across 16 text-classification tasks (14 Amazon domains plus IMDB and MR) and claims that learned shared features transfer to new tasks suggest the approach will influence how researchers design MTL and transfer systems. The method is broadly applicable within NLP and likely to be adopted or built upon, though it is not clearly transformative across all of machine learning.

reviewer_a.justification: This work tackles an important practical problem in multi-task NLP—separating shared vs task-specific features—and proposes a concrete adversarial shared-private framework that extends adversarial training from binary to multi-class task discrimination. Empirical gains across 16 text-classification tasks (14 Amazon domains, IMDB, MR) and the claim that learned shared features transfer to new tasks indicate the approach could change how researchers design MTL systems. The extension to multi-class adversarial discrimination and demonstrated cross-domain improvements are likely to be adopted and extended by others, though the approach largely combines existing ideas rather than introducing a wholly new paradigm.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of separating shared vs. task-specific features in multi-task NLP and mitigating negative transfer by proposing an adversarial shared-private MTL framework. Extending adversarial training from binary to multi-class task discrimination is a technically useful advance that enables joint training over many tasks. Empirical evidence of lower error rates across 16 text-classification tasks (14 Amazon domains plus IMDB and MR) and claims that learned shared features transfer to new tasks suggest the approach will influence how researchers design MTL and transfer systems. The method is broadly applicable within NLP and likely to be adopted or built upon, though it is not clearly transformative across all of machine learning.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper's focus—adversarial shared-private multi-task learning for text classification using shared and private LSTMs with a multi-class task discriminator—is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. It addresses the right audience by tackling multi-task representation learning and transfer, compares against appropriate baselines (FS-MTL, SP-MTL, MT-CNN, MT-DNN), and evaluates on standard datasets (14 Amazon review domains, IMDB, MR). The formulation (minimax adversarial training via gradient reversal) and evaluation metrics (error rate, cross-entropy) match norms and expectations for the community. | B(5): This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it proposes an adversarial shared-private multi-task learning framework for text classification using LSTMs and a multi-class task discriminator, a topic of clear interest to both NLP and ML communities. The methodology, use of adversarial training, and evaluation on 16 standard datasets (14 Amazon domains, IMDB, MR) with comparisons to strong MTL and single-task baselines match the methods and evaluation standards expected at leading venues. The problem formulation (separating shared vs. private feature spaces and enabling transfer) and positioning against prior work are appropriate for the audience of a top conference.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's focus—adversarial shared-private multi-task learning for text classification using shared and private LSTMs with a multi-class task discriminator—is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. It addresses the right audience by tackling multi-task representation learning and transfer, compares against appropriate baselines (FS-MTL, SP-MTL, MT-CNN, MT-DNN), and evaluates on standard datasets (14 Amazon review domains, IMDB, MR). The formulation (minimax adversarial training via gradient reversal) and evaluation metrics (error rate, cross-entropy) match norms and expectations for the community.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it proposes an adversarial shared-private multi-task learning framework for text classification using LSTMs and a multi-class task discriminator, a topic of clear interest to both NLP and ML communities. The methodology, use of adversarial training, and evaluation on 16 standard datasets (14 Amazon domains, IMDB, MR) with comparisons to strong MTL and single-task baselines match the methods and evaluation standards expected at leading venues. The problem formulation (separating shared vs. private feature spaces and enabling transfer) and positioning against prior work are appropriate for the audience of a top conference.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a broad set of relevant baselines (vanilla LSTM, BiLSTM, sLSTM, FS-MTL, SP-MTL, MT-CNN, MT-DNN) on the same datasets (14 Amazon domains, IMDB, MR) using the same evaluation metric (error rate), and reports hyperparameters—supporting fair experimental conditions. It discusses limitations of competing SP-MTL and FS-MTL formulations and clearly describes how the proposed adversarial shared-private model differs. The summary does not indicate whether the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so there may be a minor omission in reporting/qualitative comparison. Overall the comparisons are good with only minor gaps.  | B(4): The paper compares against a broad set of appropriate baselines (single-task LSTM variants, FS-MTL, SP-MTL, MT-CNN, MT-DNN) on the same datasets (14 Amazon domains + IMDB and MR) using the same metric (error rate), with hyperparameters reported, supporting fair comparisons. It also analyzes how its adversarial shared-private approach differs from FS-MTL and SP-MTL and reports lower error rates than prior multi-task baselines. However, the summary does not indicate whether the paper acknowledges cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so there may be minor omissions in discussion of competitive results.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of relevant baselines (vanilla LSTM, BiLSTM, sLSTM, FS-MTL, SP-MTL, MT-CNN, MT-DNN) on the same datasets (14 Amazon domains, IMDB, MR) using the same evaluation metric (error rate), and reports hyperparameters—supporting fair experimental conditions. It discusses limitations of competing SP-MTL and FS-MTL formulations and clearly describes how the proposed adversarial shared-private model differs. The summary does not indicate whether the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so there may be a minor omission in reporting/qualitative comparison. Overall the comparisons are good with only minor gaps.

It also analyzes how its adversarial shared-private approach differs from FS-MTL and SP-MTL and reports lower error rates than prior multi-task baselines. However, the summary does not indicate whether the paper acknowledges cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so there may be minor omissions in discussion of competitive results.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable novel combination: a shared-private LSTM MTL architecture augmented with adversarial training where the adversary is extended from binary to multi-class task discrimination to enforce task-invariant shared features. This clearly differentiates it from prior SP-MTL and fully-shared approaches by directly addressing contamination of the shared space and by formulating a multi-class adversary (implemented via gradient reversal and the combined loss L = LTask + λLAdv + γLDiff). While it builds on existing ideas (shared/private decomposition and adversarial invariance), extending adversarial training to joint multi-task discrimination and integrating it into the SP-MTL framework is a non-trivial, informative contribution for experts. | B(3): The paper proposes an adversarial shared-private MTL framework that combines a shared/private LSTM architecture with adversarial training and extends adversarial task discrimination from binary to multi-class. This differentiates it from prior FS-MTL and SP-MTL baselines and adapts known adversarial (gradient reversal) techniques to multi-task settings, which is a non-trivial engineering extension. However, the core components (shared-private decomposition and adversarial domain/task discrimination) are established ideas, so the contribution is moderate—an incremental but useful advancement rather than a highly novel paradigm shift.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable novel combination: a shared-private LSTM MTL architecture augmented with adversarial training where the adversary is extended from binary to multi-class task discrimination to enforce task-invariant shared features. This clearly differentiates it from prior SP-MTL and fully-shared approaches by directly addressing contamination of the shared space and by formulating a multi-class adversary (implemented via gradient reversal and the combined loss L = LTask + λLAdv + γLDiff). While it builds on existing ideas (shared/private decomposition and adversarial invariance), extending adversarial training to joint multi-task discrimination and integrating it into the SP-MTL framework is a non-trivial, informative contribution for experts.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes an adversarial shared-private MTL framework that combines a shared/private LSTM architecture with adversarial training and extends adversarial task discrimination from binary to multi-class. This differentiates it from prior FS-MTL and SP-MTL baselines and adapts known adversarial (gradient reversal) techniques to multi-task settings, which is a non-trivial engineering extension. However, the core components (shared-private decomposition and adversarial domain/task discrimination) are established ideas, so the contribution is moderate—an incremental but useful advancement rather than a highly novel paradigm shift.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with the standard intro → method → experiments → conclusion structure, and the main contributions (ASPMTL framework, multi-class adversarial discriminator, transferability) are stated early. Notation and method components (shared/private LSTMs, loss L = LTask + λLAdv + γLDiff) are described consistently and hyperparameters are reported, aiding reproducibility. Related work coverage is thorough and evaluation across many baselines is clear, though the summary notes only ‘adequate’ writing and does not mention figures/tables explicitly, suggesting minor presentation/detailing issues remain. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear progression from methodology (shared-private LSTM, adversarial multi-class discriminator, loss L = LTask + λLAdv + γLDiff) to experiments (16 tasks, baselines, error-rate evaluation) and conclusion. The main contributions are stated early and concretely (ASPMTL framework, multi-class adversarial discriminator, transferability). Notation and definitions seem consistent and explicit enough for reproduction (shared/private encoders, gradient reversal, reported hyperparameters), and related work coverage is thorough. However, the summary does not describe figures/tables or their effectiveness, and the writing is characterized only as “adequate,” indicating minor presentation issues remain.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized with the standard intro → method → experiments → conclusion structure, and the main contributions (ASPMTL framework, multi-class adversarial discriminator, transferability) are stated early. SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The authors evaluate on a large suite of 16 classification tasks (14 Amazon domains plus IMDB and MR) and compare to multiple strong baselines, which supports the main empirical claims. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and hyperparameters are provided, aiding reproducibility, but code is not mentioned. The summary does not indicate explicit ablation or sensitivity studies for the adversarial and difference-loss components, nor a discussion of failure cases for their own method, so the analysis is adequate but missing some important experiments and negative-result discussion. | B(4): Experiments are broad and compare against many strong baselines across 16 text-classification tasks (14 Amazon domains + IMDB + MR), and the paper reports lower error rates and transfer results, supporting the main claims. However, the summary does not indicate explicit ablation studies or sensitivity analyses of key components (e.g., adversarial loss weight λ, difference loss γ), which is an important gap. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and some limitations of alternative models (SP-MTL, FS-MTL) are discussed, but there is no clear discussion of failure cases or negative results for the proposed method and code is not mentioned (though hyperparameters are reported), which limits reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The authors evaluate on a large suite of 16 classification tasks (14 Amazon domains plus IMDB and MR) and compare to multiple strong baselines, which supports the main empirical claims. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and hyperparameters are provided, aiding reproducibility, but code is not mentioned. The summary does not indicate explicit ablation or sensitivity studies for the adversarial and difference-loss components, nor a discussion of failure cases for their own method, so the analysis is adequate but missing some important experiments and negative-result discussion.

reviewer_b.justification: Experiments are broad and compare against many strong baselines across 16 text-classification tasks (14 Amazon domains + IMDB + MR), and the paper reports lower error rates and transfer results, supporting the main claims. However, the summary does not indicate explicit ablation studies or sensitivity analyses of key components (e.g., adversarial loss weight λ, difference loss γ), which is an important gap. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and some limitations of alternative models (SP-MTL, FS-MTL) are discussed, but there is no clear discussion of failure cases or negative results for the proposed method and code is not mentioned (though hyperparameters are reported), which limits reproducibility.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by empirical evidence: the adversarial shared–private architecture with a multi-class discriminator is evaluated on 16 tasks (14 Amazon domains + IMDB + MR) and compared to reasonable baselines (FS‑MTL, SP‑MTL, MT‑CNN, MT‑DNN), with hyperparameters reported. The technical formulation (shared/private LSTMs + gradient reversal adversarial training) is standard and internally consistent, and no incorrect mathematical derivations are indicated in the summary. However, the summary does not report statistical significance, ablation details for transfer claims, or code release, leaving some experimental validation gaps and weakly supported transfer conclusions. Overall the work is technically sound with minor empirical-evidence limitations. | B(4): The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments reporting lower error rates across 16 standard text-classification datasets; the proposed objective L = LTask + λLAdv + γLDiff and use of a gradient-reversal adversarial multi-class discriminator is technically consistent with established adversarial MTL practice. There are no nontrivial mathematical derivations required beyond this formulation, and nothing in the summary indicates errors in the presented equations. The experimental setup uses appropriate baselines (FS-MTL, SP-MTL, MT-CNN, MT-DNN), standard datasets, and reports hyperparameters, but the summary omits mention of statistical significance testing and gives limited detail on how transfer-to-new-tasks was evaluated. Thus the conclusions are largely justified by the reported results, with minor concerns about the transfer claim and the lack of theoretical guarantees that task-specific information is fully removed from the shared space.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by empirical evidence: the adversarial shared–private architecture with a multi-class discriminator is evaluated on 16 tasks (14 Amazon domains + IMDB + MR) and compared to reasonable baselines (FS‑MTL, SP‑MTL, MT‑CNN, MT‑DNN), with hyperparameters reported. The technical formulation (shared/private LSTMs + gradient reversal adversarial training) is standard and internally consistent, and no incorrect mathematical derivations are indicated in the summary. However, the summary does not report statistical significance, ablation details for transfer claims, or code release, leaving some experimental validation gaps and weakly supported transfer conclusions. Overall the work is technically sound with minor empirical-evidence limitations.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments reporting lower error rates across 16 standard text-classification datasets; the proposed objective L = LTask + λLAdv + γLDiff and use of a gradient-reversal adversarial multi-class discriminator is technically consistent with established adversarial MTL practice. There are no nontrivial mathematical derivations required beyond this formulation, and nothing in the summary indicates errors in the presented equations. The experimental setup uses appropriate baselines (FS-MTL, SP-MTL, MT-CNN, MT-DNN), standard datasets, and reports hyperparameters, but the summary omits mention of statistical significance testing and gives limited detail on how transfer-to-new-tasks was evaluated. Thus the conclusions are largely justified by the reported results, with minor concerns about the transfer claim and the lack of theoretical guarantees that task-specific information is fully removed from the shared space.

main_claims: Proposes an adversarial shared-private multi-task learning framework (ASPMTL) that separates shared and task-specific latent feature spaces and uses adversarial training to prevent task-specific information contaminating the shared space.
Extends adversarial training from binary to multi-class task discrimination so multiple tasks can be jointly trained with a task discriminator acting adversarially against the shared encoder.

Demonstrates empirical improvements on 16 text classification tasks (including 14 Amazon product review domains, IMDB and MR), reporting lower error rates than single-task and prior multi-task baselines, and claims the learned shared knowledge can be transferred to new tasks.
_summary.methodology: Use a shared-private LSTM architecture where each task has a private LSTM and there is a shared LSTM; a multi-class task discriminator predicts task type from the shared representation and is trained adversarially (minimax via a gradient reversal layer) to encourage task-invariant shared features; final loss is L = LTask + λLAdv + γLDiff.
datasets: Amazon product reviews (14 domains, e.g., Books, DVDs, Electronics) extracted from Blitzer et al. (2007)
limitations: Shared-private model (SP-MTL) has no guarantee that sharable features will not reside in private space and vice versa, potentially ignoring useful sharable features.
Fully-shared model (FS-MTL) can degrade performance for some tasks because it mixes private and shared information into a unified space.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - multi-task text classification (LSTM + adversarial training)
